# Related Work

Our paper relates to four strands of literature: algorithmic collusion, platform pricing, technology-skill complementarity, and regression discontinuity methodology.

**Algorithmic collusion.** A large theoretical literature examines whether autonomous pricing agents can sustain supra-competitive outcomes. @calvano2020artificial show that Q-learning agents develop reward-punishment strategies in simulation, though @lambin2024learning argues these reflect learning inertia rather than genuine strategic collusion, and @bichler2024online find that more practical algorithms (bandits, PPO) generally converge closer to Nash equilibrium. The empirical evidence is concentrated in oligopolistic, homogeneous-good markets. @assad2024algorithmic document a 38% margin increase in German gasoline duopolies with mutual algorithmic adoption, using structural breaks to identify adopters and brand-HQ adoption shares as instruments. @musolff2022algorithmic identifies Edgeworth-cycle facilitation among Amazon repricing algorithms. @calderwang2024coordinated find evidence of price coordination through RealPage's shared algorithm in multifamily rentals. @brown2025algorithmic introduce a distinct theoretical channel --- algorithmic coercion --- whereby a single fast, committed algorithm reshapes a rival's payoffs without requiring mutual adoption. @harrington2026hub cautions that hub-and-spoke coordination is feasible even with many small firms when a single platform acts as the algorithmic "hub" --- a structure that describes Airbnb's Smart Pricing by construction. Our paper provides evidence from the other end of the market-structure spectrum: a many-seller, differentiated-product platform where, despite the hub-and-spoke architecture, the conditions for effective coordination (product homogeneity, bilateral monitoring, punishment capacity) are absent.

**Platform pricing and pricing frictions.** @huang2025pricing estimates a structural model of Airbnb pricing in San Francisco and finds that 85% of hosts set near-uniform prices due to cognitive constraints. His key counterfactual shows that the welfare-optimal platform design sets price *variation* (demand-responsive adjustments across nights and lead times) while allowing hosts to set *levels* --- nearly replicating the first-best. @foroughifar2024challenges study why only 22% of hosts adopt Smart Pricing, identifying pessimistic prior beliefs as the dominant barrier. They find that Smart Pricing generates lower revenue than manual custom pricing because it maximizes bookings rather than host profit. @zhang2025smart document that differential algorithm adoption contributes to racial revenue disparities on Airbnb. @garcia2024cognitive study analogous cognitive frictions in hotel pricing. We complement this literature by providing quasi-experimental evidence of the *market-level equilibrium* effects of algorithm availability, which the structural models take as an input rather than estimating directly.

**Technology-skill complementarity.** Our finding that algorithm effects concentrate among sophisticated hosts connects to the canonical task framework of @autor2003skill, which predicts that information technology complements nonroutine analytical tasks while substituting for routine ones. @bresnahan2002information extend this to three-way complementarity between IT, worker skill, and organizational practices. @brynjolfsson2025generative find the opposite pattern for generative AI in customer service --- AI disproportionately benefits low-skill workers --- consistent with AI substituting for routine interpersonal tasks rather than complementing analytical ones. Our setting maps to the complementarity case: dynamic pricing is a nonroutine analytical task where the algorithm amplifies pre-existing expertise. @mackowiak2009optimal provide the specific mechanism via rational inattention: the algorithm relaxes hosts' information-processing constraints, enabling demand-responsive pricing that was previously too costly to implement manually.

**Regression discontinuity in time.** Our estimation procedure draws on @hausman2018regression, who caution that time-based RDD requires careful seasonal adjustment, and on @noack2025flexible, who show that flexible (ML-based) covariate adjustments in RDD produce consistent estimates regardless of specification and can reduce confidence interval length by up to 30%. We use @calonico2014robust for bandwidth selection and bias-corrected inference, and @cattaneo2020density for density tests. Our multi-city design estimates the common-cutoff RDD separately in each of eight markets, providing replication across diverse settings and enabling leave-one-city-out robustness checks. The year-over-year DiD design provides a complementary identification strategy that differences out calendar-date-specific confounders.
